chapter_info.subject: Mathematics
chapter_info.chapter_name: Pair of Linear Equations in Two Variables

points: A pair of linear equations in two variables can be solved graphically by plotting both equations as lines on a coordinate plane and finding their point(s) of intersection.

For equations a₁x + b₁y + c₁ = 0 and a₂x + b₂y + c₂ = 0: if a₁/a₂ ≠ b₁/b₂, lines intersect (unique solution, consistent); if a₁/a₂ = b₁/b₂ = c₁/c₂, lines coincide (infinitely many solutions, dependent); if a₁/a₂ = b₁/b₂ ≠ c₁/c₂, lines are parallel (no solution, inconsistent).
The graphical method provides geometric visualization but may be impractical for non-integral coordinates.
The substitution method: Express one variable in terms of the other from one equation, substitute into the second equation, solve for one variable, then find the other.

The elimination method: Multiply equations by suitable constants to make coefficients of one variable equal, then add or subtract to eliminate that variable and solve the resulting single-variable equation.
Both methods handle special cases: infinitely many solutions (true statements with no variable) and no solution (false statements with no variable).

Real-world situations (cost problems, age problems, digit problems, work problems) can be reduced to pairs of linear equations by selecting appropriate variables and translating conditions into equations.

A pair of equations is consistent (has a solution), inconsistent (no solution), or dependent (infinitely many solutions). The algebraic and graphical methods confirm these classifications through coefficient ratios.
The choice of solving method depends on equation form and computational convenience, but all methods yield the same solution for consistent systems.
